ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for hotplate saba and chicken set; closest parent 'fish and meat mixed dish' used as ontology anchor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for teppan-style saba and chicken set meal; A0CKP (fish and meat mixed dish) used as closest parent.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard set meal portion comprising approximately 100 g grilled saba fillet, 100 g grilled chicken, and 200 g steamed white rice, totalling approximately 400 g.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated composite from USDA FoodData Central: Atlantic Mackerel (FDC ID 175139), Chicken breast grilled (FDC ID 331960), and Cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756)
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for hotplate saba and chicken set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA FoodData Central. Values estimated as a weighted composite: 25% saba (grilled Atlantic mackerel), 25% grilled chicken breast, and 50% cooked white rice by weight, reflecting a typical 100 g saba + 100 g chicken + 200 g rice set meal. Per-100 g values derived by dividing composite totals by 4 (400 g total serving). Saba values from USDA FDC ID 175139 (Atlantic mackerel, cooked, dry heat); chicken from FDC ID 331960 (chicken breast, grilled); rice from FDC ID 169756 (rice, white, cooked). Sodium adjusted upward by approximately 150 mg per 100 g to account for soy-based sauce typical of teppan-style preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of white rice and mixed rice-based meals — analogous estimate based on cooked white rice GI (Atkinson et al., 2008, American Journal of Clinical Nutrition)
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for hotplate saba and chicken set. The GI is estimated at 64 based on the dominant carbohydrate component: cooked white rice (GI 64, glucose reference, Atkinson et al. 2008, AJCN). Saba and chicken contribute negligible glycemic carbohydrates. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: (GI 64 × carbohydrate_g per serving 46.25 g) / 100 = 29.6. Carbohydrate per serving = 18.5 g per 100 g × 400 g serving / 100 = 74 g total; however, rice portion (200 g) contributes approximately 46.25 g carbohydrate (23.1 g per 100 g cooked rice × 2). GL = (64 × 46.25) / 100 = 29.6.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy-based teppan sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit this dish to occasional consumption and request reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation, as the estimated sodium content of approximately 320 mg per 100 g (approximately 1280 mg per full set serving) approaches or exceeds 50% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium glycemic index driven by white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should be aware that the white rice component contributes a medium GI of approximately 64 and a high glycemic load per serving (GL ~30); substituting brown rice or reducing rice portion size can meaningfully lower postprandial glucose response, while the saba and chicken components provide beneficial protein and omega-3 fatty acids that support glycaemic management.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial omega-3 fatty acids from saba offset by elevated sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saba (mackerel) is a rich source of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA) which support cardiovascular health and reduce triglycerides; however, the high sodium content from teppan sauce is a concern for blood pressure management in individuals with cardiovascular disease, and portion control with reduced-sodium sauce is advised.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Hotplate Saba and Chicken Set (铁板鲭鱼鸡肉套餐) is a Japanese-influenced teppan-style set meal popular in Singapore's food delivery and casual dining scene. The dish features a grilled saba (mackerel) fillet and grilled chicken pieces cooked on a sizzling iron hotplate, served alongside steamed white rice. The hotplate cooking method imparts a characteristic smoky, caramelised crust to both the fish and chicken, while the interior remains moist and tender. The dish is typically seasoned with a soy-based sauce, sesame oil, and garnished with spring onion, producing a savoury, umami-rich flavour profile. Saba is a nutritionally dense oily fish providing high-quality protein (approximately 20 g per 100 g raw), long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), vitamin B12, vitamin D, and selenium. Chicken breast contributes lean protein with low saturated fat. The white rice component provides the primary carbohydrate source with a medium glycemic index (GI ~64). The combined set meal delivers approximately 168 kcal per 100 g, with 13.5 g protein, 4.8 g fat, and 18.5 g carbohydrate per 100 g. Clinically, the omega-3 content from saba is beneficial for cardiovascular health and triglyceride reduction, but the sodium content from soy-based sauce (estimated ~320 mg per 100 g) is a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. For individuals with Type 2 Diabetes, the medium-GI white rice component results in a high glycemic load per full serving (GL ~30), and portion reduction or rice substitution is advisable. The dish is dairy-free and suitable for pescatarian-adjacent diets, though it contains fish, soy, sesame, and gluten allergens.
common_user_queries: Calories in saba and chicken set meal
Is saba fish good for diabetics?
Is teppan saba high in sodium?

confidence_reason: No exact entry for hotplate saba and chicken set exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional source. Nutrient values are estimated as a weighted composite of USDA FoodData Central entries for grilled Atlantic mackerel, grilled chicken breast, and cooked white rice, with sodium adjusted for soy-based sauce. GI is estimated from the white rice component using a peer-reviewed source. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach and the absence of a direct analytical measurement for this specific dish.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from USDA FoodData Central (FDC IDs 175139, 331960, 169756) weighted at 25% saba, 25% chicken, 50% rice by mass, reflecting a 100 g saba + 100 g chicken + 200 g rice set meal. 2. Sodium is adjusted upward by approximately 150 mg per 100 g to account for soy-based teppan sauce; actual sodium will vary significantly by restaurant preparation. 3. trans_fat_g is 0.0 (confirmed zero for grilled fish and chicken with no hydrogenated fat components per USDA FoodData Central source data). 4. GI of 64 is an analogous estimate based on the white rice component (Atkinson et al. 2008, AJCN); no direct GI measurement exists for this composite dish. 5. The field dataed in the source (Korean Cuisine - Funan Mall) serves this dish despite the Japanese cuisine classification; the dish is a Japanese-influenced teppan preparation common across Japanese and Korean casual dining in Singapore. 6. Halal status is marked false as it cannot be confirmed without restaurant-specific certification; consumers requiring halal certification should verify with the serving restaurant. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from the USDA FoodData Central composite and are approximate; no sentinel -1 values are used as reasonable estimates are available from the approved source components.
